title: Allan dismisses fears that state lacks power for data centre surge
full_text:
Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan has insisted the state's investments in renewable energy will be enough to meet the demands of the data centres her government wants to lure to the state, despite new forecasts warning the sector's power consumption is growing faster than expected.
Allan, who has sought to position Victoria as the national leader in building the infrastructure that powers artificial intelligence, said data centres were critical to the state's economic prosperity and her government was bringing more renewable energy into the system to power them.
"Investors from around the globe see Victoria as a great place to invest and there's billions and billions of dollars of investments coming into the data sector alone because we have the land, the workforce and energy security going forward," she said on Thursday.
"We're ensuring that there is energy security ahead, whether it's for data centres, whether it's for businesses ... and for households [and it] is a core part of our work in driving more renewable energy to come into our mix."
The Australian Energy Market Operator updated its long-term outlook at a monthly meeting of industry and consumer participants on Wednesday.
The rising strain on the grid as demand for AI processing and cloud computing grows was discussed as AEMO reported that data centres would consume one-eighth of current electricity use in a decade, after demand in Victoria almost doubled in the past year.
Underlying demand for electricity from Victoria's 30 data centres jumped 94 per cent to 187 megawatts in the three months to March 31 compared with a year earlier, AEMO data shows.
In NSW, which has twice as many operational data centres as Victoria, demand rose 18 per cent to 398 megawatts over the same period.
Nationally, the average monthly demand for data centres in the first quarter was relatively low at 586 megawatts, less than 3 per cent of total demand.
But demand will keep rising as the 90 data centre projects in the pipeline - some 74 of which are in NSW and Victoria - come online, on top of 162 existing facilities, London-based research group DC Byte said.
Data centres are the physical infrastructure that powers modern digital connectivity and are critical to every use of services such as ChatGPT, Netflix or work video call applications.
Each centre requires exorbitant amounts of energy and water and this poses a complicated problem for governments juggling competing forces, such as the impact of electricity costs on the cost of living and the goal of reducing carbon emissions.
The reliability of Victoria's future energy supply has been questioned due to coal-fired power stations being scheduled to retire in the next few years, the flow of gas slowing from the Bass Strait - the main source for the east coast for decades - and delays to transmission projects to connect renewable energy to the grid.
Allan, on Thursday, said Victoria had the lowest wholesale electricity prices in the national market because it was building more renewable energy projects that would drive prices down even further.
A report by the Victorian Auditor-General's Office last year found the state had not adequately planned for the renewable energy transition and could not guarantee energy reliability. It said while Victoria was on track to meet its 2025 renewable energy use targets, the targets of achieving 65 per cent by 2030 and 95 per cent by 2035 were less certain.
summary: Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan reassured that the state's renewable energy investments will support the growing demand from data centres, despite concerns about energy supply. Data centre power use in Victoria has surged, with a forecast that it could consume one-eighth of the state's electricity in ten years.
author: Sumeyya Ilanbey and Jenny Wiggins
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Jacinta Allan emphasizes Victoria's renewable energy plans.
Data centre power demand in Victoria rose 94% in three months.
AEMO predicts data centres will consume 12.5% of electricity in a decade.
Concerns exist about future energy supply reliability.
Victoria aims for 65% renewable energy by 2030.
ticker_line: Victoria backs renewable energy for data centres
